name: Write Prompt
description: turn a vague goal into a precise, reusable prompt that gets the output you want
when_to_use: when the user wants help writing or improving a prompt for an AI model
body:
1. nail the goal first: what output, in what format, for what reader.
2. set role + task + constraints in that order, drop the pleasantries.
3. show the shape of the answer, not just describe it (a stub, a header, a schema).
4. state hard limits explicitly: length, what to exclude, what to never assume.
5. dry-run it once in your head, cut any line that doesn't change the output.
6. leave one slot the user fills per use so it's reusable, not one-shot.

name: Design Agent
description: spec an autonomous agent: its role, tools, loop, and stop conditions
when_to_use: when the user wants to build an AI agent that acts in steps with tools
body:
1. write the one-sentence job and the single success metric before anything else.
2. list the tools it gets, and for each, when NOT to call it.
3. define the loop: observe, decide, act, check, repeat — name the exit condition.
4. add guardrails: max steps, irreversible-action confirms, what to never touch.
5. decide what it does when stuck: retry, ask, or bail — pick one, write it down.
6. specify the final handoff format so the caller can consume it.

name: Structured Output
description: force model output into a strict, parseable schema
when_to_use: when the user needs the model to return json or a fixed format a program will parse
body:
1. define the exact schema: every key, its type, required vs optional.
2. give one filled example, then say 'output only this, no prose'.
3. pin down enums and formats (dates, ids, units) so values aren't free text.
4. say what to put when a value is unknown (null, empty string — pick one).
5. add a guard: if it can't comply, return an error object, never half-valid data.
6. validate the result against the schema before trusting it downstream.

name: Few-Shot Examples
description: pick and order examples that teach the model the pattern by demonstration
when_to_use: when a task is easier to show than describe, or the model keeps missing the format
body:
1. choose 2-5 examples that cover the real range, including one tricky edge.
2. make every example identical in structure so the pattern is unmistakable.
3. order easy-to-hard, put the most representative one last (recency bias).
4. include a near-miss with its correction if a specific error keeps recurring.
5. keep inputs realistic, not toy — the model copies the vibe, not just the form.
6. end with the live input in the exact same shape as the examples.

name: Critique Answer
description: review an AI-generated answer for errors, gaps, and unsupported claims
when_to_use: when the user wants a second pass over an answer before they trust or ship it
body:
1. read it once for what it claims, ignore the tone.
2. check each factual claim: supported, plausible, or invented.
3. hunt for the silent gap — the question it dodged or half-answered.
4. test the logic: does the conclusion actually follow from the steps.
5. list issues by severity, wrong-facts first, nitpicks last.
6. give the one fix that most improves it, not a rewrite.

name: Reduce Hallucination
description: rework a prompt so the model invents fewer facts
when_to_use: when the model keeps making up facts, citations, or details
body:
1. give it the source material instead of asking from memory.
2. add an explicit out: 'say i don't know if it's not in the source'.
3. ban unsourced specifics — names, numbers, urls, quotes — unless grounded.
4. ask it to cite the exact span it used for each claim.
5. split recall from reasoning: get the facts first, then reason in a second pass.
6. verify the riskiest claim independently before relying on the whole answer.

name: Chain Of Thought
description: get the model to reason step by step before answering, then separate work from result
when_to_use: when a task needs multi-step reasoning, math, or careful logic and the model rushes
body:
1. ask it to think through the steps before stating the answer.
2. break the problem into named sub-steps so each is checkable.
3. make it show intermediate values, not just the final number.
4. have it sanity-check the result against the question before committing.
5. put the final answer on its own line, clearly fenced from the working.
6. for hidden reasoning, ask only for the answer plus a one-line justification.

name: Refine Iteratively
description: improve an output over rounds instead of regenerating from scratch
when_to_use: when a first draft is close but the user wants it better through feedback loops
body:
1. keep the draft, change one dimension per round, not everything at once.
2. give feedback as concrete edits, not vibes ('cut intro', 'add a number here').
3. tell it what to keep, models often discard the good parts on a rewrite.
4. diff each round against the last so progress is visible.
5. stop when the marginal change stops helping — name that bar up front.
6. lock the final version and restate why it's done.

name: Set Persona
description: give the model a role and voice that fits the audience and task
when_to_use: when the user wants the output to sound like a specific expert, tone, or character
body:
1. pick the role for the expertise it implies, not for flavor.
2. define the audience — the persona only matters relative to who's reading.
3. set 3 concrete voice traits (terse, blunt, no jargon) over adjectives like 'professional'.
4. give a one-line sample of the target voice as a calibration anchor.
5. state what the persona must never do (no hedging, no disclaimers, no lists).
6. keep expertise honest — a persona doesn't make invented facts true.

name: Decompose Task
description: split a big request into ordered subtasks the model can do reliably
when_to_use: when a single prompt is too big and the model drops parts or loses the thread
body:
1. list every distinct thing the request actually asks for.
2. order them by dependency — what must finish before the next can start.
3. make each subtask have one clear output that feeds the next.
4. run them as separate prompts, passing each result forward.
5. keep a running checklist so nothing silently gets skipped.
6. assemble the pieces and check the whole against the original ask.

name: Constrain Output
description: bound length, scope, and style so the model stops over-answering
when_to_use: when responses are too long, off-topic, or padded with filler
body:
1. set a hard ceiling: word count, bullet count, or sentence count.
2. scope it: 'only X, do not cover Y or background'.
3. ban the usual padding — preambles, summaries, 'it's important to note', caveats.
4. demand the answer lead with the conclusion, not build up to it.
5. if it must choose, tell it to pick one and not list alternatives.
6. check the result against the ceiling and cut, don't accept overflow.

name: Debug Prompt
description: diagnose why a prompt gives wrong, inconsistent, or off-format output
when_to_use: when a prompt isn't working and the user doesn't know which part is at fault
body:
1. reproduce the bad output once so you're fixing the real failure.
2. classify it: wrong facts, wrong format, wrong scope, or refusal.
3. strip the prompt to its core and add back one instruction at a time.
4. check for conflicts — two instructions fighting cause flaky output.
5. fix the single highest-impact line, re-run, confirm the change.
6. lock in the fix and note what broke it so it doesn't regress.

name: LLM As Judge
description: set up the model to score or rank outputs against a rubric
when_to_use: when the user wants to auto-evaluate or compare AI outputs at scale
body:
1. write the rubric as 3-5 criteria with explicit pass/fail or a numeric scale.
2. define each score level concretely so two judges would agree.
3. give the judge the criteria, the input, and the output — nothing it can bias on.
4. require a reason per score before the score, not after.
5. for pairwise, randomize order to kill position bias, judge once each way.
6. spot-check the judge against a few human-rated cases before trusting it.

name: Ground With Context
description: feed the model the right source material so answers stay anchored to truth
when_to_use: when answers must come from specific documents, data, or a knowledge base (rag)
body:
1. retrieve only the passages that actually bear on the question, drop the rest.
2. label each chunk with its source so citations are traceable.
3. put the question and the instruction after the context, not before.
4. tell it to answer only from the provided context, not prior knowledge.
5. handle the empty case: 'if the context doesn't cover it, say so'.
6. have it quote or cite the chunk backing each claim.
